title: Pork Gyoza

overview.description: Japanese pan-fried dumplings filled with seasoned pork, cabbage, spring onion, ginger and garlic, wrapped in homemade gyoza dough.

method: Mix the flour and salt in a bowl.
Gradually add the boiling water, stirring with chopsticks or a fork until a shaggy dough forms.
When cool enough to handle, knead for 5–10 minutes until smooth.
Cover and rest for at least 30 minutes.
Place the shredded cabbage in a bowl and mix with the salt.
Leave for 10 minutes, then squeeze firmly to remove as much liquid as possible.
Combine the pork, cabbage, spring onions, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar and white pepper.
Mix in one direction until the filling becomes sticky and cohesive.
Divide the dough into approximately 30 equal pieces.
Roll each piece into a ball.
Flatten each ball and roll into a thin circle approximately 9–10cm in diameter.
Keep the wrappers covered with a damp cloth while working.
Place approximately 1 tablespoon of filling in the centre of each wrapper.
Moisten the edge with water.
Fold the wrapper over the filling and pleat the edge to seal.
Repeat with the remaining wrappers and filling.
To cook, heat the oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat.
Arrange the gyoza flat-side down in the pan.
Cook until the bases are golden brown.
Add the water and immediately cover with a lid.
Steam for 5–7 minutes until the filling is cooked through.
Remove the lid and continue cooking until the remaining water has evaporated and the bases are crisp.

learning.did-you-know: Although gyoza originated from Chinese jiaozi, the Japanese version developed its own thinner wrapper, finely minced filling and characteristic pan-fried base.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make dumpling wrappers from scratch, control moisture in a filling and fold and cook filled dumplings.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A brilliant batch-cooking project that teaches several useful skills while producing a freezer full of genuinely convenient homemade snacks.
chapman-tips: Squeeze as much liquid as possible from the cabbage. Wet filling makes the wrappers difficult to seal.
Keep unused wrappers covered so they do not dry out.
The filling should be sticky and cohesive before you start wrapping.
Don't overfill the wrappers. A smaller, neatly sealed gyoza is better than a bursting one.
The easiest way to learn pleating is to practise with a small amount of filling first.
The classic cooking method gives you both a crisp base and a soft, steamed top.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze uncooked gyoza in a single layer on a tray until solid, then transfer to a freezer bag or container. Cook directly from frozen using the same pan-frying and steaming method, adding several minutes to the steaming time.
notes.leftovers: Cooked gyoza can be refrigerated and reheated in a frying pan, although freshly cooked gyoza have the best texture.
